evidence_basis_summary: Author-defined inclusion rules filter countries (elections in period, >=100 msgs/party), parties (two largest pre-election), and messages (no retweets/URLs, >=100 chars) - a light but explicit human adjudication step (C001_S1 pp.1184).
notes: Upgraded from low/not_applicable: extracted text documents explicit country/party/message inclusion filters, i.e., observable adjudication work even if modest.

evidence_basis_summary: Task is pure text classification of party affiliation with no causal/mechanism claim anywhere in the intro, task, or methods sections (C001_S1 pp.1181-1187).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence is confirmable across full intro and methods.

evidence_basis_summary: The construct (politician's actual party) is fixed a priori as binary objective ground truth - humans set a narrow pre-specified label space rather than eliciting perspectival variation (C001_S1 pp.1184 'offers a ground truth').
notes: Upgraded from medium: full methods section directly supports this as a construct-boundary-setting case with deliberately narrow construct.

evidence_basis_summary: Single corpus used (van Vliet et al. 2020 parliamentarian tweets), deliberately filtered to one message type; no pluralist corpus strategy (C001_S1 pp.1184).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence confirmed against full methods.

evidence_basis_summary: Humans design the prompt template, pin the model version (gpt-4-0314), set sampling (250/party, 500 balanced), run Krippendorff alpha reliability tests, and embed MTurk attention-check controls (C001_S1 pp.1185-1186).
notes: Upgraded from low/partial-unclear to yes/robust: methods section documents an extensive protocol/provenance layer that clearly persists.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper explicitly probes cross-country/cross-language transportability ('performance is lower outside the United States... still achieves relatively high accuracy') and uses Supplementary S1 to test transfer to non-ground-truth users (C001_S1 pp.1183-1184).
notes: Upgraded from low/not_applicable: transportability across 11 countries is an explicit design feature, making this role partially observable.

evidence_basis_summary: No fieldwork, interviewing, or situated interpretation - annotation is on archived Twitter messages with no human-in-the-loop interlocution (C001_S1 pp.1184-1186).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence confirmed against full task/methods description.

evidence_basis_summary: Paper flags epistemological risks and 'pitfalls and best practices' for LLM annotation and explicitly discusses who the coders are (experts, MTurk, LLM), but labor-allocation implications are only gestured at, not systematized (C001_S1 pp.1183).
notes: Upgraded from low: abstract and discussion framing raise accountability concerns in passing; full discussion section not in stored text.

evidence_basis_summary: Binary party label space is fixed in advance by the researcher and encoded in the prompt ('Respond either [party1] or [party2]'), a direct instance of construct-boundary setting (C001_S1 p.1186).
notes: Upgraded from medium: the verbatim prompt in methods directly shows the construct boundary being set by the human.

evidence_basis_summary: Objective ground-truth design deliberately collapses annotator disagreement into a single correct label; no subgroup/perspective preservation (C001_S1 pp.1184 'bypasses... treating human annotation as the ground truth').
notes: Upgraded from low/partial-unclear: the paper's entire framing is that perspective pluralism is not the point; this is a clean not_applicable.

evidence_basis_summary: Cross-country generalization across 11 languages/contexts is the paper's central transportability claim, and SI S1 tests transfer to users without public ground truth (C001_S1 pp.1183-1184).
notes: Upgraded from low/not_applicable: the cross-country design is explicitly a transportability test.

evidence_basis_summary: Comparisons use accuracy/macro-F1/Krippendorff alpha against known party labels, but there is no simulator calibration or matching to an external reference distribution - ground truth is the construct itself (C001_S1 pp.1186-1187).
notes: Upgraded from low: methods section confirms benchmarking is against ground-truth labels, not calibration in the codebook's sense.

evidence_basis_summary: No interviews, fieldwork, or interactional data generation (C001_S1 pp.1184-1186).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence confirmed from full task/methods text.

evidence_basis_summary: No tacit/archival contextual interpretation work by humans - contextual inference is exactly what the LLM is tested on and said to do well (C001_S1 pp.1181 abstract, pp.1184).
notes: Upgraded from low: paper frames contextual inference as a capacity being displaced to the LLM, not preserved as a human role.

evidence_basis_summary: No triage/screening stage in the pipeline - sampling is random from a filtered corpus, not human-ranked (C001_S1 pp.1184).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence confirmed against full methods.

role_label: Routine coder on high-consensus, ground-truth-rich tasks
evidence_basis_summary: GPT-4 reaches 0.934 accuracy and 0.982 inter-coder reliability on the US case, outperforming MTurk, expert PhD coders, Naive Bayes, and BERT - a direct empirical case of routine coders shrinking toward auditors (C001_S1 abstract + pp.1187).
notes: Upgraded from medium: the paper's headline result is precisely this role's shrinking, directly evidenced in the stored full text.

evidence_basis_summary: No writing/drafting task in scope - paper is about annotation, not prose generation (C001_S1 pp.1181-1187).
notes: Upgraded from low: absence confirmed against full scoped task.
